title: Homeostasis — keeping conditions constant
examTip: Homeostasis is a guaranteed B5 question. AQA wants you to define it precisely, explain why it matters, describe the role of receptors/control centres/effectors, and give examples (temperature, blood glucose, water balance). The negative feedback loop is a core target — understand it mechanically, not just as a phrase.
introTips: Homeostasis sounds complicated but it's just your body keeping things steady — and there's a very logical system behind it.
Every homeostatic mechanism has the same structure: a detector, a control centre, and an effector. Once you see that pattern, all the examples make sense.
Negative feedback is the key concept — know it well enough to apply it to any example AQA gives you, not just temperature or glucose.
intro: Your body works best within very precise limits. Too hot and your enzymes denature. Too much glucose and your cells are damaged. Too little water and your reactions slow. Homeostasis is the set of processes your body uses to detect when things drift off-balance and correct them — automatically, continuously, without you having to think about it. Understanding the mechanism is what earns marks, not just knowing the word.

keyPoints.heading: What homeostasis is and why it matters
keyPoints.content: Homeostasis is the regulation of the internal conditions of a cell or organism to maintain optimum conditions for function, in response to internal and external changes. Your body needs to keep: core temperature around 37°C (so enzymes work at their optimal rate), blood glucose concentration within a narrow range (so cells always have enough energy), and water and ion balance (so cells don't shrink or burst). If any of these drift outside normal range, enzyme activity is disrupted — reactions slow, proteins denature, and cells malfunction.
writeBullets: Homeostasis = regulation of internal conditions to maintain optimum conditions for function
Core temperature kept at ~37°C — enzymes work at optimal rate
Blood glucose kept in a narrow range — cells have energy, avoid damage
Water and ion balance maintained — prevents cells shrinking or bursting
If conditions drift outside normal range, enzymes are disrupted and cells malfunction

keyTerms.def: The regulation of internal conditions of a cell or organism to maintain optimum conditions, despite changes inside and outside the body
The conditions inside the body — temperature, pH, glucose concentration, water levels — that homeostasis maintains

keyPoints.memTip: Homeostasis = keeping it constant. The three things to keep constant for AQA: temperature (~37°C), blood glucose (narrow range), water/ion balance. Always link temperature to enzyme activity — that's why it matters.

keyPoints.heading: The control system: receptor, control centre, effector
keyPoints.content: All homeostatic control systems have three components. A receptor detects a change in the internal environment (e.g. a thermoreceptor in the skin or hypothalamus detects temperature change). The receptor sends information to a control centre (usually the brain or a specific part of the brain like the hypothalamus) which compares the reading to the normal level (the set point) and sends instructions. An effector (a muscle or gland) carries out the corrective response. This system is automatic and continuous — the body is always monitoring and adjusting.
writeBullets: Receptor: detects change in the internal environment
Control centre: compares reading to set point, sends corrective instructions
Effector: muscle or gland that carries out the corrective response
Set point: the normal target value the body tries to maintain
The system is automatic and continuous — always monitoring and adjusting

keyTerms.def: A cell or organ that detects a change in the internal environment and sends information to the control centre
The part of the body (often the brain) that receives information from receptors, compares it to the set point, and coordinates the response
A muscle or gland that carries out a response to bring conditions back to the set point
The normal, optimum level of a variable (e.g. 37°C for body temperature) that homeostatic mechanisms work to maintain

keyPoints.memTip: Receptor → Control Centre → Effector. RCE. The control centre is always comparing 'what IS it?' to 'what SHOULD it be?' — that comparison is the heart of homeostasis.

keyPoints.heading: Negative feedback — the correction mechanism
keyPoints.content: Negative feedback is the mechanism that keeps conditions near the set point. When a variable moves away from normal, the change is detected, and the response works to return the variable back towards the set point — opposing the change. For example: if body temperature rises, receptors detect this, the control centre triggers cooling responses (sweating, vasodilation), and as temperature falls back towards 37°C, the cooling responses are reduced. If temperature drops, warming responses (shivering, vasoconstriction) are triggered instead. The word 'negative' means the response is in the opposite direction to the change — it negates the change.
writeBullets: Negative feedback: change detected → response in OPPOSITE direction → variable returns to set point
Response is reduced as the variable approaches normal — preventing overcorrection
Temperature too high → sweat, vasodilate → cool down → reduce cooling response
Temperature too low → shiver, vasoconstrict → warm up → reduce warming response
Called 'negative' because the response negates (opposes) the original change

keyTerms.def: A control mechanism where a change from the set point triggers a response that returns the variable back towards the set point, opposing the change
Widening of blood vessels near the skin surface — increases heat loss by radiation
Narrowing of blood vessels near the skin surface — reduces heat loss, conserving body heat

keyPoints.memTip: Negative feedback = opposite direction to the change. Draw a simple loop: too high → response to lower it → approaches normal → response reduced. Too low → response to raise it → approaches normal → response reduced. The key is that the correction always works against the change.

keyPoints.heading: Temperature regulation — thermoregulation
keyPoints.content: The hypothalamus in the brain is the control centre for body temperature. It contains thermoreceptors that detect the temperature of blood flowing through it. The skin also has thermoreceptors that detect external temperature. When body temperature rises: sweat glands produce more sweat (evaporation cools the skin), blood vessels in the skin vasodilate (more blood flows near the surface, more heat is radiated), and hairs lie flat (reduces insulating air layer). When body temperature falls: sweat production decreases, blood vessels vasoconstrict (less blood near the surface, less heat lost), muscles shiver (generate heat by respiration), and hairs stand up (trap an insulating layer of air).
writeBullets: Hypothalamus = control centre for temperature (contains thermoreceptors)
Too hot: sweat produced, vasodilation, hairs lie flat
Too cold: sweating stops, vasoconstriction, shivering, hairs stand up
Sweating cools by evaporation — heat is transferred from skin to sweat
Vasodilation = more blood near skin surface → more heat radiated; vasoconstriction = opposite

keyTerms.def: Region of the brain that acts as the temperature control centre — detects blood temperature and coordinates thermoregulation responses
The process of maintaining core body temperature at ~37°C through homeostatic mechanisms
Rapid, involuntary muscle contractions that generate heat through respiration, helping raise body temperature

keyPoints.memTip: Too hot = sweat, vasodilate, lie flat. Too cold = stop sweating, vasoconstrict, shiver, stand up. The direction of hair and blood vessels always makes logical sense — trap heat when cold, lose heat when hot. The hypothalamus controls all of it.

tables.title: Thermoregulation responses compared
rows: Stand up — traps warm air layer

0.question: Which of the following is the best definition of homeostasis?
options: The process of digesting food and releasing energy
The regulation of internal conditions to maintain optimum conditions for function
The removal of waste products from the body
The control of movement and coordination by the nervous system
0.correctFeedback: ✅ Exactly right — that's the AQA definition. The key words are 'regulation', 'internal conditions', and 'optimum' — use them all when writing it in an exam.
0.wrongFeedback: Homeostasis specifically means regulating the internal conditions (temperature, glucose, water) to keep them at optimum levels for cells to function. It's not about digestion, excretion, or movement control — it's about maintaining a stable internal environment.

1.question: What is negative feedback?
options: A response that amplifies a change away from the set point
A response that returns a variable towards the set point by opposing the original change
The process by which receptors detect changes in the external environment
A type of hormonal signal that increases when conditions are normal
1.correctFeedback: ✅ Correct — negative feedback is always in the opposite direction to the change, pushing the variable back towards normal.
1.wrongFeedback: Negative feedback works by opposing the original change and returning the variable to its set point. If temperature rises, negative feedback triggers cooling; if it falls, negative feedback triggers warming. The response always works against the direction of change.

3.question: When body temperature rises above normal, which TWO responses occur?
3.correctFeedback: ✅ Right — vasodilation increases heat loss from the skin surface, and sweating cools by evaporation. Both work to lower temperature.
3.wrongFeedback: When too hot, the body needs to LOSE heat. Vasodilation increases blood flow near the skin surface (more heat radiated), and sweating increases (evaporation cools the skin). Vasoconstriction and shivering are responses to cold — they'd make things worse if you were already too hot.

commonMistakes: Confusing vasodilation with vasoconstriction — vasodilation = widening = more heat lost (response to being too hot). Vasoconstriction = narrowing = less heat lost (response to being too cold). Draw the blood vessel cross-section both ways to visualise it.
Saying 'the blood gets hotter' during vasodilation — it doesn't. More warm blood flows near the skin surface, so more heat is radiated away from the body. The blood itself cools as it loses heat.

Forgetting the hypothalamus as the control centre — AQA frequently asks which part of the brain controls temperature. It's the hypothalamus, and it works because thermoreceptors detect the temperature of blood flowing through it.
Describing negative feedback as a one-off correction — it's continuous. The body is constantly monitoring and constantly making small adjustments.
Saying sweating 'removes heat' — it's more precise to say evaporation of sweat from the skin surface transfers heat energy away from the body, cooling the skin.
examTips: When asked to define homeostasis, include: (1) regulation of internal conditions, (2) to maintain optimum conditions, (3) in response to internal and external changes. All three parts together get full marks.

For negative feedback questions, always state the direction: 'the response works to return the variable back towards the set point by opposing the change'. Just saying 'negative feedback' without explaining it usually isn't enough.
When asked to explain thermoregulation, name the hypothalamus as the control centre. Describing 'the brain' without naming the hypothalamus may not earn the mark.

AQA sometimes gives a novel context (e.g. a new animal or a drug that blocks a receptor) and asks you to apply homeostasis principles. Use the receptor → control centre → effector → negative feedback framework even in unfamiliar situations.
revisionCardBullets: Homeostasis = regulation of internal conditions to maintain optimum conditions for function
Three things regulated: core temperature (~37°C), blood glucose, water/ion balance
Control system: receptor → control centre → effector
Set point = normal target value the body tries to maintain
Negative feedback: change detected → response in opposite direction → returns to set point
Temperature control centre = hypothalamus (in the brain)
Too hot: vasodilation + sweating + hairs lie flat
Too cold: vasoconstriction + shivering + hairs stand up
Vasodilation = widening of blood vessels → more heat radiated
Vasoconstriction = narrowing of blood vessels → less heat lost
